name: Webster Tarpley
description: Webster Tarpley (1946-2021) was an American author, economist, and political activist known for his conspiratorial views on historical and contemporary events. He promoted a wide range of theories, often focusing on elite power structures and their alleged manipulation of global politics. Tarpley's work often centered on accusations against powerful families, intelligence agencies, and international organizations, claiming they orchestrate events to establish a New World Order. He argued that events like the September 11 attacks, the Oklahoma City bombing, and various political assassinations were false flag operations carried out by these shadowy groups to further their agendas. His narratives often involve claims of population control, financial manipulation, and the suppression of technological advancements. Mainstream historians and political analysts generally dismiss Tarpley's theories as lacking credible evidence and relying on selective interpretation of facts, logical fallacies, and unsubstantiated claims. They criticize his methodology for prioritizing pre-conceived conclusions over objective analysis and for failing to meet the standards of academic rigor. His work has been described as anti-Semitic and anti-American by some critics. While Tarpley gained a following among those skeptical of mainstream narratives, his views remain highly controversial and are not supported by established scholarship.
subtitle: Conspiracy theorist and political activist

claims.claim: 9/11 was an inside job orchestrated by elements within the US government and intelligence agencies.
claims.evidence: Collapse of WTC 7, alleged foreknowledge by government officials, inconsistencies in the official narrative.
claims.criticism: The 9/11 Commission Report and other investigations have concluded that al-Qaeda was responsible. Structural engineering experts attribute the collapse of WTC 7 to uncontrolled fires caused by debris from the Twin Towers.

claims.claim: The Bilderberg Group and other elite organizations secretly control global politics and economies.
claims.evidence: Annual meetings held in private, perceived influence of members in government and business.
claims.criticism: While these groups undoubtedly exert influence, there is no credible evidence of a secret agenda to control the world. Meetings are often for discussion and networking, not decision-making.

claims.claim: Various historical events, including political assassinations, were false flag operations designed to manipulate public opinion.
claims.evidence: Alleged anomalies and inconsistencies in official accounts, circumstantial evidence.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians argue that these claims lack concrete proof and rely on speculation and misinterpretation of historical records. Alternative explanations are often more plausible and supported by stronger evidence.

keyFigures.name: Chip Berlet
keyFigures.role: Critic and researcher of conspiracy theories

mythologyConnections: Shadow Government (parallels to deities controlling human affairs from behind the scenes)
Apocalypse (Tarpley's narratives often imply a coming crisis orchestrated by elites)

timeline.event: Tarpley authors books and articles promoting his conspiracy theories.

sources.author: Chip Berlet
